title: Goat bloat: recognition and first response
subtitle: Identifying frothy and free-gas bloat and what to do before the vet arrives.
excerpt: Bloat in goats builds fast and can be fatal within hours if not treated. The left flank distends above the hipbone, the animal is uncomfortable and stops eating, and in severe cases she may have difficulty breathing. Knowing the type of bloat and acting promptly gives the vet the best chance of success.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Goats. AHDB Dairy goat health management. Merck Veterinary Manual, Bloat in Ruminants, merckvetmanual.com.

glossaryTerms.definition: Bloat caused by the rumen contents forming a stable foam that traps gas and prevents normal belching. Usually triggered by lush legume-rich pasture or sudden diet changes.
Bloat where gas accumulates in the rumen as a single pocket rather than foam. Often caused by an obstruction in the oesophagus or a posture problem preventing the animal from eructating.
The first and largest chamber of a goat's stomach, where plant material is fermented by microorganisms. Gas produced during fermentation is normally released by belching.

content.text: Bloat is a veterinary emergency. If the left flank is distended and the animal is showing distress, your first action is to phone your vet while you assess the situation. What you do in the meantime depends on whether you are dealing with

sits on the left side of the body. In bloat, the left flank, the hollow triangle between the last rib, the hipbone, and the spine, becomes distended and tight. In severe cases the bulge extends beyond the hipbone.

Tap the left flank with your knuckle. Free-gas bloat produces a drum-like hollow sound. Frothy bloat produces a dull thud because the gas is trapped in foam rather than pooled above the rumen contents.

Free-gas bloat can sometimes be resolved by walking the animal on a slope with her head uphill, which repositions the gas pocket relative to the cardia. Do this while you wait for the vet. Do not try to tube a ruminant yourself unless you have been shown the technique by your vet. A stomach tube in the wrong place is dangerous.
Immediate management while waiting for the vet

Keep the animal on her feet if possible. A goat in lateral recumbency with bloat is at serious risk of inhaling rumen contents if she vomits. Walk her slowly; do not leave her unsupervised if distress is severe.

For frothy bloat, simethicone (an anti-foaming agent available from veterinary suppliers) can be given by mouth while you wait. The dosage is on the product label; it is not harmful in overdose. Simethicone breaks down the foam and allows the gas to escape by normal belching.

Most frothy bloat cases happen when goats reach lush pasture on an empty stomach. Legume-rich pasture (clover, sainfoin, lucerne) is highest risk, especially in the early morning when the plants are wet with dew. Give hay before turning out. Limit access to legume-heavy pasture in the first few hours after dawn. Avoid sudden pasture changes.

Free-gas bloat is less predictable and often has a positional or mechanical cause, an oesophageal obstruction, a postural problem, or a side effect of other illness. Any goat who shows bloat more than once deserves a vet assessment of the underlying cause.
